title: Story — Principles of Screenwriting
author: Robert McKee
source: Story: Substance, Structure, Style, and the Principles of Screenwriting, 1997.

lineage.note: The philosophical heavyweight. Core concept: the GAP between expectation and result is where meaning is generated. Conflict is non-negotiable for McKee, which is exactly where Kishōtenketsu pushes back.

stages.name: Inciting Incident
stages.function: Upsets the balance of life and triggers the spine. The protagonist now has an object of desire.

stages.name: Progressive Complications
stages.function: Escalating forces of antagonism. Each complication harder than the last; the protagonist's tools become inadequate.

stages.name: Crisis
stages.function: The ultimate decision under maximum pressure. The protagonist must choose between irreconcilable goods or the lesser of two evils.

stages.name: Climax
stages.function: The result of the crisis decision plays out; story value tips definitively.

applicability.limitations: Insists on conflict as the engine; bad fit for material that resists antagonism (Kishōtenketsu's domain). McKee's seminars are also famous for being prescriptive about the 'crisis decision' moment — not every film has one.
